title: Garage Door Won't Close All the Way — Causes & Fixes
question: My garage door starts to close then reverses and goes back up, or won't close from the opener at all. What's wrong and what can I safely fix myself?
summary: When a garage door reverses instead of closing, the opener's safety system is doing its job — and the cause is almost always the photo-eye sensors near the floor being misaligned, dirty, or blocked. Realign and clean them first; that fixes most cases. If the door binds, drags, or slams down, the problem is the rollers/track or the springs — and springs are the one part you should never touch yourself.

commonCauses.cause: Photo-eye safety sensors are misaligned, dirty, or blocked. The two small sensors near the bottom of each track must 'see' each other; if the beam is broken or off-axis, the opener refuses to close and reverses.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Look at the sensor LEDs. Most setups show a steady light on both when aligned; a blinking or off LED means misaligned or blocked. Check for a broom, cobweb, or sun glare in the beam path.

commonCauses.cause: Close-limit / travel setting is off. The opener thinks the door has reached the floor before it actually does (or overshoots), so it stops short or reverses. Common after a power loss or opener replacement.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Watch where the door stops. If it consistently stops a few inches short or hits the floor then bounces back up, the limit screw or travel setting needs adjustment (see the opener's manual).

commonCauses.cause: Rollers, hinges, or track are dry, bent, or obstructed, so the door binds and the opener senses resistance and reverses.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Pull the red release cord to disconnect the opener and lift the door by hand. It should glide and stay put halfway. If it's heavy, sticks, or jerks, the track/rollers (or springs) need attention.

commonCauses.cause: Broken or weak torsion/extension spring. The door feels very heavy by hand, slams down, or won't stay halfway. The opener can't overcome the imbalance.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Disconnected from the opener, a balanced door is light and holds halfway. If it crashes down or you see a visible gap in the coiled spring above the door, the spring is the problem — do not operate it and do not DIY.

fixes: Start with the photo eyes. Wipe both lenses with a soft dry cloth and clear anything in the beam path. Make sure neither was bumped out of position.
Realign the eyes: loosen the wing nut/bracket, aim the sensors straight at each other, and adjust until both LEDs are steady. Tighten the brackets without nudging them.
Test close. If it now closes, you're done. If it still reverses with clean, aligned eyes, move on.

Pull the red emergency-release cord and lift the door by hand to feel for binding. If it glides freely and holds halfway, the issue is the opener's travel/limit setting — adjust per the opener manual's close-limit instructions in small increments.
If the door binds or drags by hand, inspect the track for dents and the rollers/hinges for damage; lubricate rollers, hinges, and springs with a garage-door lube (not WD-40). Re-test.
If the door is heavy, slams down, won't stay halfway, or you see a broken spring or frayed cable — stop. Leave the opener disconnected and call a garage-door technician.
Re-engage the opener by pulling the release cord back toward the door and running the opener once.
diyVsPro: DIY for the safe 90%: cleaning and aligning the photo eyes, clearing obstructions, lubricating rollers and hinges, and adjusting the opener's travel limits per the manual. Call a professional immediately for anything involving the springs or lift cables — torsion and extension springs store enormous energy and cause severe injuries when handled without the right tools and training. Also call a pro for bent tracks, a door off its track, or an opener that grinds or won't respond at all.
typicalCostUSD: $0 to realign sensors or adjust limits; $10-$20 for garage-door lubricant. Professional spring replacement runs $150-$350; roller or cable replacement $100-$250; a new opener installed $300-$600.
timeEstimate: 10-30 minutes for sensor alignment, cleaning, and lubrication.
safetyNote: Never adjust, loosen, or replace garage-door springs or cables yourself — they are under extreme tension and are a leading cause of serious home-repair injuries. The photo-eye and lubrication steps here are safe DIY; spring, cable, and off-track work are for a trained technician only. Always disconnect the opener before lifting the door by hand, and keep hands clear of section joints and the spring assembly.
toolsParts: Garage-door lubricant (silicone or lithium spray, not WD-40)
Phillips/flat screwdriver and a small wrench (for sensor brackets)
The opener's owner manual (for travel/limit settings)
searchTerms: garage door won't close all the way
garage door closes then opens back up
garage door reverses before hitting the floor
garage door sensor blinking won't close
how to align garage door safety sensors
garage door won't close from remote
garage door closes then bounces back up
sources: U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission guidance on automatic garage-door opener safety and photo-eye reversing systems
Major opener manufacturer (Chamberlain/LiftMaster, Genie) troubleshooting and travel-limit instructions
International Door Association safety guidance on garage-door spring and cable hazards
disclaimer: General guidance only. Garage-door springs and cables are dangerous and are not a DIY repair; consult a qualified garage-door technician for anything beyond sensor, lubrication, and opener-setting adjustments.
verifierNote: Drafted from CPSC/opener-manufacturer practice; spring/cable work explicitly routed to a pro. Pending human source verification.
